other_observers: Seen with Nathan Pieplow, Mark Peterson, Peter Gent, and John Drummond; found by Rich Miller. Also seen by Bill Maynard, Gene Rutherford, Brandon Percival, and SeEtta Moss.
specific_location: the MacKenzie Avenue bridge over the Arkansas River
description: the bird was a fairly large hawk, about the size of Red-tailed Hawks seen soon before the observation. The shape was fairly typical of a Buteo, with a short neck, fairly large head, broad and long wings, and chunky body. The bird flew with powerful wingbeats, a little faster than Red-tailed Hawk.  The plumage was entirely black, except for the tail, which had a bold and wide white basal tail band, and a wide (but less so than the basal band) black subterminal band, and a narrow white terminal band. The tail was extremely short for a hawk, and was broad, especially when fanned out in flight. The wings were extremely broad, much more so than any other buteo I've seen.  The legs were long for a Buteo, with long tarsi that were bright yellow. The cere was bright yellow, and the bill and eye were black.Behaviors: On the first day I saw the bird, I observed it for only 3 seconds or so, flying down the river at a distance. I was only able to observe the tail pattern during this observation.  On the second day, we first saw the bird fly up to the exposed perch about 80 yards away. Right after it first landed it lost its balance, tiltling forward and fanning its tail, giving us an excellent look at the tail pattern and uppertail coverts. It only stayed on the perch for about 10 seconds before flying into and through the trees, and then down the line of trees to the west. It flew across the gap created by the road, allowing for excellent if short looks at it in flight at about 50 yards. It was last seen flying to the west outside the trees, but likely dived back into the trees at some point.Call: none heardPlumage: adult
